1: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה (vay'daber YHWH el-moshe): 'YHWH spoke to Moses.' The standard revelation formula introduces a lengthy liturgical legislation. The placement is deliberate: immediately after Joshua's commissioning (Numbers 27), YHWH gives Israel the complete offering calendar. The new leader will inherit not only authority but a fully specified liturgical system — the rhythm of sacrifice that structures Israel's time.
2: קָרְבָּנִי לַחְמִי לְאִשַּׁי (qorbani lachmi le'ishai, H7133+H3899+H801): 'my offering — the food of my fire offerings.' Qorban (offering, H7133) derives from qarav (draw near): the offering is what brings Israel near to YHWH and YHWH's presence near to Israel. Lechem (food/bread) here is anthropomorphic — YHWH describes the offerings as 'my food,' language also found in Ezekiel. It is not literalism but a relational idiom: the offerings are the table-fellowship of the covenant. בְּמוֹעֲדוֹ (bemoe'do, H4150): 'at its appointed time' — the offerings must be presented precisely at the times YHWH has designated. Mo'ed governs the entire calendar of worship.
3: עֹלַת תָּמִיד (olat tamid, H5930+H8548): 'the regular/perpetual burnt offering.' Tamid (continual, regular, perpetual) — the unbroken daily rhythm of sacrifice that marks Israel's ongoing covenant relationship. Olah (burnt offering) derives from alah (go up): the entire animal ascends to YHWH in smoke. Benei shanah temimim (H8549) — 'yearling lambs without blemish': the offering must be at the peak of physical perfection, a recurring requirement that will find its ultimate expression in the NT's 'lamb without blemish' (1 Peter 1:19).
4: אֶת-הַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד תַּעֲשֶׂה בַבֹּקֶר (et-hakeves ha'echad ta'aseh veboqer): 'the one lamb in the morning.' The twice-daily sacrifice establishes morning and evening as the fixed poles of Israel's liturgical day. בֵּין הָעַרְבַּיִם (bein ha'arbayim, H6153): 'between the evenings' — the period of late afternoon when the sun begins to descend. The same phrase governs the time of the Passover slaughter (Exodus 12:6). The daily rhythm of Israel's sacrifice is synchronized with the rhythms of light itself: dawn and dusk as the natural clock of covenantal worship.
5: עִשָּׂרוֹן סֹלֶת (issaron soleth, H6241+H5560): 'one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour.' The minhah (grain offering, H4503) accompanies the animal sacrifice. Soleth (fine flour) — the highest-grade flour, sifted. שֶׁמֶן כָּתִית (shemen katit, H8081+H3795): 'pressed/beaten oil' — olive oil produced by the first pressing, the purest grade. The specificity of quality (fine not coarse flour; pressed not crushed oil) signals that the best of Israel's agricultural produce belongs at YHWH's table. Worship is not a residual claim on what remains after human needs are met.
6: עֹלַת תָּמִיד הָעֲשֻׂיָה בְּהַר סִינַי (olat tamid ha'asuyah behar sinai): 'the regular burnt offering, made at Mount Sinai.' The authority for the daily sacrifice is grounded explicitly in the Sinai revelation — the apex of YHWH's self-disclosure to Israel. This is not a new institution but the perpetuation of what was established at the moment of covenant-making. The re'ach nichoach (H7381+H5207, 'pleasing aroma') — a sensory-theological idiom throughout the sacrifice legislation: the offerings please YHWH not as literal nutrition but as a relational gesture of devotion ascending to him.
7: שֵׁכָר (shekhar, H7941): 'fermented/strong drink.' The drink offering is poured out in the holy place. Shekhar (usually 'strong drink,' distinct from yayin/wine) appears in cultic contexts as a libation. בַּקֹּדֶשׁ הַסֵּךְ (baqodesh hassekh): 'pour it out in holiness' — the holy place is the site of the libation, emphasizing that even the liquid poured on the ground is a sacred act. The drink offering is not merely incidental but integral to the complete sacrificial rite.
8: כְּמִנְחַת הַבֹּקֶר וּכְנִסְכּוֹ (keminchat haboqer ukhnisc'o): 'like the grain offering of the morning and its drink offering.' The evening sacrifice precisely mirrors the morning — same animal, same grain offering, same drink offering. The symmetry creates a complete liturgical frame: morning and evening sacrifices bracket Israel's day with covenantal worship. The repetition formula signals that the tamid is not two distinct rites but one continuous act spread across the day.
9: וּבְיוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת (uvyom hashabbat): 'on the Sabbath day.' The Sabbath sacrifice doubles the daily offering: two lambs instead of one, two-tenths of flour instead of one. The Sabbath is not a suspension of sacrifice but an amplification — rest for labour and increase in worship. The Sabbath olah is presented al-olat hatamid (in addition to the regular burnt offering): the Sabbath offering does not replace the daily sacrifice but layers on top of it. Mishpatam — 'their due' — each offering has its proper, prescribed measure.
10: עֹלַת שַׁבַּת בְּשַׁבַּתּוֹ (olat shabbat b'shabbato): 'the Sabbath burnt offering on its [every] Sabbath.' The doubled noun emphasizes regularity: every Sabbath, without exception, has its specific offering. The phrase milevad olat hatamid v'niskah — 'in addition to the regular burnt offering and its drink offering' — establishes the structural principle governing the entire calendar: each higher-order occasion adds offerings to the base of the perpetual daily sacrifice, never replacing it. The liturgical calendar builds upward from the tamid as its foundation.
11: וּבְרָאשֵׁי חָדְשֵׁיכֶם (uvra'shei chodsheikhem, H7218+H2320): 'at the beginning of your months / at your new moons.' Rosh chodesh (new moon, lit. 'head of the month') marks the lunar calendar's reset: the first appearance of the new moon. The New Moon offering is substantially larger than the daily or Sabbath sacrifice: two young bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs. The new moon was a significant commercial rest-day in Israel (Amos 8:5) as well as a liturgical occasion. Its offerings signal that the lunar calendar itself is ordered by YHWH's appointment.
12: שְׁלֹשָׁה עֶשְׂרֹנִים (sheloshah esronim, H6241): 'three-tenths of an ephah.' The grain-offering proportions scale with the animal: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram (Numbers 28:12), one-tenth per lamb (Numbers 28:13). The graduated scale expresses proportional honour — larger animals bring larger grain tributes. Soleth belulah vashemen (fine flour mixed with oil) — the standard formula for the minhah; the mixing of grain and oil is a consistent cultic requirement throughout the sacrificial legislation.
13: עִשָּׂרוֹן עִשָּׂרוֹן לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (issaron issaron lakeves ha'echad): 'one-tenth for each individual lamb.' The double noun (issaron issaron) distributes the measure: one-tenth per lamb, separately. The seven lambs each receive their own portion. The meticulous proportionality of the sacrifice system — animals and their grain and drink offerings precisely calibrated — reflects a theology of ordered worship: YHWH's service is not improvised but specified, and precision in worship honours the God who specifies it.
14: נִסְכֵּיהֶם (niskeihem, H5262): 'their drink offerings.' The New Moon drink offering specification: half a hin of wine (yayin, H3196) per bull, a third per ram, a quarter per lamb. Here yayin (wine) replaces shekhar (Numbers 28:7's strong drink) — the New Moon gets wine. Zot olat chodesh b'chodsho — 'this is the burnt offering of each month at its month' — the closing formula for the New Moon legislation.
15: וּשְׂעִיר עִזִּים אֶחָד לְחַטָּאת (use'ir izzim echad lachatta't, H8163+H5795+H2403): 'one male goat as a sin offering.' The chatta't (H2403, sin offering) — from chata' (to miss the mark, fail, sin) — makes corporate kipper (atonement) for the community each month. The monthly sin offering acknowledges that over the course of a month Israel accumulates corporate moral debt that requires expiation. The goat is offered milevad olat hatamid — in addition to the perpetual offering — underlining that the sin offering supplements rather than substitutes the daily burnt offering.
16: פֶּסַח לַיהוָה (pesach la-YHWH, H6453): 'Passover to YHWH.' Pesach — the term for the festival and for the sacrificial animal — derives from pasach (H6452): to pass over (spare), or possibly to protect by hovering. The Passover falls on the 14th of the first month (Nisan/Abib), marking the anniversary of the exodus. The brevity of this verse — two words plus a date — is notable: the Passover itself needs no extended legislative treatment here because it was fully legislated in Exod 12. This is a calendar entry, not a new ordinance.
17: וּבַחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה חָג (uvachamisha asar yom lachodesh hazeh chag, H2282): 'on the 15th of this month is the feast.' Chag (H2282, feast, pilgrimage feast) — from chagag (to celebrate, dance in a circle). The three chagim (Passover/Unleavened Bread, Weeks, Tabernacles) were pilgrimage festivals requiring appearance at the sanctuary. Matzot (H4682, unleavened bread) — eaten for seven days from the 15th: the departure bread that commemorates the hasty exodus, leaving no time for dough to rise (Exodus 12:39).
18: בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ (bayom harishon miqra-qodesh, H4744+H6944): 'on the first day a holy assembly/convocation.' Miqra (H4744) — sacred assembly, convocation, that which is called together — from qara (to call, proclaim). The holy assembly marks the first and seventh days of Unleavened Bread as days of complete liturgical community. כָּל-מְלֶאכֶת עֲבֹדָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ (kol melechet avodah lo ta'asu): 'no laborious work shall you do' — the festival days share the Sabbath's prohibition on regular work, though the exact scope differs.
19: וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם אִשֶּׁה (vehiqravtem isheh, H7126+H801): 'you shall present a fire offering.' The Passover week burnt offering is substantial: two bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs — all without blemish. The festival-specific offering stacks atop the daily and monthly offerings that continue throughout. The number of animals reflects the importance of the occasion: Passover week is the highest point of Israel's liturgical year, commemorating YHWH's act of redemption from Egypt.
20: מִנְחָתָם (minchatam, H4503): 'their grain offering.' The grain offering proportions for Passover week: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram. These proportions are consistent with the New Moon offerings (Numbers 28:12-13), establishing a standard measure that applies across major festivals. Consistency in proportions reflects a theology of ordered worship: the same God who created cosmic order (Gen 1) structures his worship with mathematical precision.
21: עִשָּׂרוֹן תַּעֲשֶׂה לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (issaron ta'aseh lakeves ha'echad): 'one-tenth shall you make for each lamb.' The seven yearling lambs each receive one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour. The repeated specification (lashivat hak'vasim, for the seven lambs) ensures no lamb is overlooked in the offering system. Precision in worship is itself a form of honour: each animal receives its proper due, not a collective approximation.
22: וּשְׂעִיר-עִזִּים אֶחָד לְחַטָּאת (use'ir-izzim echad lachatta't): 'one male goat as a sin offering.' The chatta't accompanies every major festival offering in the calendar. Its consistent presence signals that no festival — not even the joyous Passover — is approached without an acknowledgment of communal sin and the need for atonement. The festival's joy and the sin offering's gravity coexist: Israel celebrates YHWH's saving acts while simultaneously acknowledging its own inadequacy before him.
23: מִלְּבַד עֹלַת הַבֹּקֶר (milevad olat haboqer): 'in addition to the morning burnt offering.' The milevad ('besides/in addition to') principle structures the entire sacrificial calendar: higher-order occasions accumulate offerings on top of the base daily sacrifice, never replacing it. The morning tamid continues to be offered even during Passover week; the festival offering is additional, not substitutionary. This layering reflects the principle that special occasions intensify rather than replace ordinary covenantal worship.
24: כָּכָה תַּעֲשׂוּ לַיּוֹם שִׁבְעַת יָמִים (kakah ta'asu layom shivat yamim): 'in this manner you shall do each day for seven days.' The offering pattern established for the first day of Unleavened Bread is repeated each day through the seven-day festival. Lechem isheh re'ach nichoach la-YHWH — 'the food of a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to YHWH' — the relational vocabulary of worship persists throughout the repetition. Worship is not diminished by regularity; the tamid is the tamid precisely because it is repeated without variation.
25: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי מִקְרָא-קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם (uvayom hashvi'i miqra-qodesh yihyeh lakhem): 'on the seventh day a holy assembly shall be for you.' Unleavened Bread ends as it began — with a holy assembly and cessation of laborious work. The seven-day festival is bracketed by miqra-qodesh on its first and last days, creating a liturgical frame. The week of unleavened bread, like the first creation week, ends with a day set apart for sacred assembly.
26: וּבְיוֹם הַבִּכּוּרִים (uvyom habikkurim, H1061): 'on the day of firstfruits.' Shavuot (Festival of Weeks) — fifty days after Passover — is introduced as the day of bikkurim (firstfruits, H1061). Minchah chadashah la-YHWH — 'a new grain offering to YHWH' — at Weeks, the new harvest's firstfruits are formally presented: the first grain of the new crop is YHWH's before any human consumption. Miqra-qodesh again — the pilgrimage feast day is a holy assembly, requiring cessation of laborious work.
27: וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם עוֹלָה לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (vehiqravtem olah lere'ach nichoach): 'you shall present a burnt offering as a pleasing aroma.' Shavuot's offering matches the Passover week structure: two bulls, one ram, seven yearling lambs without blemish. The offerings for Israel's major agricultural festivals are standardized at this level — a consistency that ties together the three chagim into a single liturgical system.
28: מִנְחָתָם (minchatam): 'their grain offering.' The Shavuot grain offering proportions are identical to those for Passover week (Numbers 28:20): three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram. The standardization makes liturgical memory possible: once a worshipper learns the proportions for one festival, they know them for all. The precision of the sacrificial system serves the worshipper's formation as well as YHWH's honor.
29: עִשָּׂרוֹן לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (issaron lakeves ha'echad): 'one-tenth for each lamb.' For the seven Shavuot lambs, one-tenth of an ephah per lamb — consistent with all other festivals. The text's near-mechanical repetition of proportions is a feature, not a flaw: the precision is itself the content. YHWH's instructions are to be followed exactly, not approximately, because worship is a covenantal act where deviation from specification is covenant-infidelity.
30: שְׂעִיר עִזִּים אֶחָד לְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם (se'ir izzim echad lekapper aleikhem, H3722): 'one male goat to make atonement for you.' Kipper (H3722, to atone, cover, expiate) — the theological core of the sin offering. The goat's blood and flesh perform the expiation of communal sin at each festival. The phrase lekapper aleikhem ('to make atonement for/on behalf of you') emphasizes that the atonement's benefit is corporate — the whole community is in view, not merely the individual offerers.
31: מִלְּבַד עֹלַת הַתָּמִיד וּמִנְחָתוֹ תַּעֲשׂוּ (milevad olat hatamid uminchat'o ta'asu): 'in addition to the regular burnt offering and its grain offering you shall make.' The closing formula for the Weeks legislation. Temimim yihyu lekhem v'niskeihem — 'they shall be without blemish for you, and with their drink offerings.' The blemish-free requirement extends to the tamid as well. The chapter closes with a comprehensive statement: every festival offering stacks on the perpetual daily sacrifice, and all must be without blemish.
